figures.gloss: The great cosmogonic genealogy chant of some 2,000 lines, composed for the chief Ka-ʻI-ʻi-mamao: the world unfolds out of Pō, the deep primordial night, beginning with the coral polyp and climbing through paired sea- and land-creatures, plants, gods, and finally chiefs — each living thing begotten in sequence, the cosmos a single descending whakapapa from darkness into the day-world (Ao).
figures.facet: Creation as genealogy rather than fiat: nothing is made, everything is begotten, so that coral, fish, pig, and king are literally kin born in ordered succession out of the same generative Night — the universe is one family tree and rank is a place upon it.
figures.provenance: The Kumulipo, preserved and transmitted within the family of King Kalākaua, first published by Kalākaua in 1889 and translated by his sister Queen Liliʻuokalani (1897); translated and exhaustively annotated by Martha Warren Beckwith, 'The Kumulipo: A Hawaiian Creation Chant' (1951).

figures.gloss: The boundless generative darkness from which all things issue and into which the dead return — not empty void but a fertile, teeming Night out of which the slime, the coral, the gods, and the chiefly line are successively born; the ancestral source-realm whose opposite and offspring is Ao, the realm of light and the living.
figures.facet: The origin is a womb-dark, not a chaos to be defeated: Pō is not slain or ordered from outside but gives birth from within itself, and remains the abode of gods and ancestors — so the night is never overcome, only complemented by day, and the lineage of the living reaches unbroken back into it.
figures.provenance: Pervasive in the Kumulipo (the first seven of sixteen wā unfold within Pō); discussed in Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology' (1940), ch. on Pō and Ao.

figures.gloss: The leading creator-god of the great quartet, lord of light, of fresh flowing water, of the forest and of living things; he who calls the first man from the red earth, whose name marks east and the rising sun, and whose waters (ka wai a Kāne) are the very principle of life. Of the high gods he asks no human sacrifice — his are the offerings of the awa-bowl and the firstfruits.
figures.facet: The supreme maker is the god of the LIVING and of fresh water specifically, set against his own dark-sea complement Kanaloa: Hawaiian theology splits the creative principle into the sunlit, life-giving Kāne and the deep, salt, shadowed Kanaloa, so the cosmos is governed by a sky-and-fresh-water god who is half of a bound pair.
figures.provenance: Davida Malo, 'Hawaiian Antiquities (Moʻolelo Hawaiʻi)'; S. M. Kamakau, 'Ka Poʻe Kahiko'; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. 'Kane and Kanaloa'.

figures.gloss: God of the ocean, the deep currents, the squid and octopus, and the far foundations of the sea — the bound companion of Kāne, journeying with him across the islands opening springs of fresh water with their digging-sticks. The two are named together in the same breath; Kanaloa is the salt deep to Kāne's sweet water, the dark to his light.
figures.facet: The sea-deep is conceived as a god's COMPANION rather than his enemy: where many traditions cast the primal waters as the slain adversary of the maker, Hawaiian thought pairs the ocean-lord with the creator as his inseparable fellow-traveller — the deep is brother, not dragon.
figures.provenance: Malo; Kamakau; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. 'Kane and Kanaloa'. A later, Christian-influenced strand recasting Kanaloa as an adversary/'Hawaiian devil' is widely judged a post-contact distortion.

figures.gloss: The great god of war and of the upright — of building, fishing, planting in their forceful aspects, and of the island-snatching rule of chiefs (Kū-kāʻili-moku, 'Kū the snatcher of islands', the war-god of Kamehameha). His worship demanded the luakini temple and human sacrifice; his many bynames govern canoe-hewing, sorcery, and the wild upland forest.
figures.facet: Force itself deified across its whole range — war, but equally the strenuous male labour of felling, fishing, and raising — so that Kū is less a thunder-god than the principle of the UPRIGHT and the exerted, the standing strength that builds and kills alike, demanding the highest sacrifice.
figures.provenance: Malo; Kamakau; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology'. The Kū-kāʻili-moku cult under Kamehameha I is richly documented in early-19th-c. accounts.

figures.gloss: God of agriculture, of rain and the fertilising cloud, of the gourd and the sweet potato, of peace, medicine, and sport; lord of the Makahiki, the great four-month festival of the year's turning when war is forbidden, tribute gathered, and games held under his white-cloth banner. He departs over the sea and is awaited again with the winter rains.
figures.facet: The god of PEACE and plenty bracketed off in his own season: Hawaiian time is cut so that for a third of the year Kū's war is laid aside and Lono's fertility, sport, and tribute reign — the dying-and-returning cloud-god of the Makahiki gives the calendar its alternating pulse of war and peace.
figures.provenance: Malo's account of the Makahiki is the locus classicus; Kamakau; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology'. Captain Cook's 1779 arrival during the Makahiki and his identification with Lono is the famous (and debated) historical episode.

figures.gloss: The volcano goddess who dwells in the fire-pit of Halemaʻumaʻu on Kīlauea; she who voyaged from Kahiki driven by her sea-sister, dug her firepits down the island chain, and in molten rage and creative fury both destroys the land and makes it new. Her family — sisters, brothers, the youngest Hiʻiaka — fill the great Pele-and-Hiʻiaka epic.
figures.facet: The land-maker is the land-DESTROYER in one body: Hawaiian cosmology grounds an actively volcanic homeland in a goddess whose every eruption both buries and creates, so that the islands themselves are her ongoing, visible work — destruction and genesis as a single cyclic act under the people's feet.
figures.provenance: The Pele-and-Hiʻiaka cycle survives in many 19th–20th-c. Hawaiian-language texts (Hoʻoulumāhiehie, Poepoe, Emerson's 'Pele and Hiiaka' 1915); Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', the Pele chapters.

figures.gloss: Pele's fierce elder sister, goddess of the sea and water, whose anger at Pele drove the fire-goddess from their homeland Kahiki across the ocean from island to island; the salt water that forever pursues and quenches her sister's fire — the two locked in the elemental quarrel of sea against volcano.
figures.facet: The sea personified specifically as the volcano's HUNTING rival: where Kanaloa's deep is a companion, Nāmaka's ocean is the lifelong adversary of the fire-mother — Hawaiian thought stages its sea-and-fire dyad as a sibling feud, the surf eternally chasing the lava that builds new shore.
figures.provenance: Central to the Pele migration legend; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', Pele chapters; the Pele-and-Hiʻiaka epic texts.

figures.gloss: The great feminine, named in countless legends across Polynesia — here the woman who, wearied of beating kapa (bark-cloth) for her family, climbs the rainbow or the moonbeam to the moon and lives there still, beating her tapa, the marks on the moon's face her work; mother in many tellings of the demigod Māui. Her name is bound to silver, to the moon, to the tides.
figures.facet: The feminine principle lifted into the MOON by an act of self-removal: Hina ascends not as a queen but as a tired working woman who simply leaves for the sky — the lunar goddess as the everywoman who chose the moon over the kapa-board, her labour made visible in its shadows.
figures.provenance: Hina legends are pan-Polynesian; Hawaiian versions in Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. 'Hina Myths'; W. D. Westervelt, 'Legends of Maʻui'.

figures.gloss: The small, late-born demigod and trickster of all Polynesia: who fished the islands up from the sea-floor with his hook Mānaiakalani; who snared the sun at Haleakalā and beat it until it agreed to move slowly so kapa could dry and food could cook; who won fire from the mud-hens (or from his ancestress Mahuika) for humankind, and died at last attempting to win immortality.
figures.facet: The benefactor who is a CHEAT and is killed by his last theft: Māui hauls up land, slows the sun, and steals fire by trickery rather than power — the culture-hero as undersized prankster — and his single failure, the attempt to defeat death, fixes mortality on humankind forever.
figures.provenance: The Māui cycle is shared across Polynesia with remarkable fidelity; Hawaiian forms in Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', the Māui chapter, and Westervelt, 'Legends of Maʻui' (1910).

figures.gloss: Mana is the sacred, efficacious power that flows through gods, chiefs, places, and things in differing measure; the kapu (tabu) system is the body of sacred prohibition that channels and protects it — the rules of separation (men's and women's eating, the chief's shadow, the off-limits season or precinct) by which mana is kept from spilling or being polluted. Together they make holiness a real, ranked, dangerous force.
figures.facet: Sacredness rendered as MEASURABLE power proportional to genealogical descent: a chief's mana is literally his nearness to Pō and the gods in the chant, so rank, taboo, and the right to rule are one continuous physics of the holy — the kapu the engineering that keeps that power safe.
figures.provenance: Malo and Kamakau on the kapu and the aliʻi; Valerio Valeri, 'Kingship and Sacrifice' (1985); the wider Polynesian 'mana'/'tapu' literature (Codrington's original 'mana' report concerns Melanesia; the Hawaiian usage is treated by Valeri and Beckwith).

figures.gloss: Pele's youngest and most beloved sister, carried to Hawaiʻi from Kahiki as an egg and warmed in her breast; heroine of the great Pele-and-Hiʻiaka epic, sent to fetch the chief Lohiʻau from Kauaʻi, on which journey she heals the sick, chants the mele that move the land and sea, and slays the moʻo who block her path. Patroness of hula alongside Laka.
figures.facet: Her power runs entirely through chant rather than force: Hiʻiaka defeats monsters, calms surf, and revives the dead by the efficacy of spoken mele — the tradition's clearest case of the word itself as a weapon and a medicine.
figures.provenance: The Pele-and-Hiʻiaka cycle: Nathaniel Emerson, 'Pele and Hiiaka: A Myth from Hawaii' (1915); the fuller Hawaiian-language newspaper serials of Hoʻoulumāhiehie (1905-6) and Moses Manu; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', the Pele chapters.

figures.gloss: A kupua demigod born in pig form to a chiefess and able to shift between hog, human, and plant shapes; god of wild boars, rain-fed agriculture, and the windward valleys, whose destructive youth, feuds with Kamehameha's ancestor Olopana, and stormy courtship-and-war with Pele are among the longest cycles in the corpus.
figures.facet: The wet, windward principle personified against Pele's dry fire: Kamapuaʻa's pursuit of Pele across the islands, repelled at last by Kapo's decoy, stages the geography of Hawaiʻi itself as an ongoing contest between rain-fed growth and volcanic heat.
figures.provenance: Thomas Thrum, 'Hawaiian Folk Tales' (1907), the Kamapuaʻa cycle; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. 'Kamapuaa Legends'; Lilikalā Kameʻeleihiwa's scholarship on the Pele-Kamapuaʻa cycle.

figures.gloss: The earth-mother who with Wākea bears the Hawaiian Islands and the founding ancestors of the chiefly lines in the Papa-and-Wākea genealogical cosmogony, a rival cosmogonic strand to the Kumulipo; her name anchors aliʻi claims to land and rank by direct descent from earth itself.
figures.facet: Land and lineage are the same claim: Papa does not merely inhabit the islands, she births them, so that a chief's right to rule a given island is, in this cosmogony, literally a birthright traced to her body.
figures.provenance: Malo, 'Hawaiian Antiquities'; Kamakau, 'Ka Poʻe Kahiko'; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. on Wākea and Papa; the genealogy is central to the aliʻi mele koʻihonua chants distinct from the Kumulipo line.

figures.gloss: Sky-father and ancestor of chiefs whose union with Papa begins the chiefly genealogies; when Papa is away he lies also with Hoʻohōkūkalani, whose stillborn first son is buried and becomes the first taro (Hāloa-naka), founding the kalo-origin story and the descent-line of Hawaiian aliʻi.
figures.facet: The sky-father's infidelity, not his sovereignty, is the load-bearing episode: it is the affair with Hoʻohōkūkalani, not the marriage to Papa, that produces the taro-and-humanity charter myth on which the whole ethic of mālama ʻāina (care for the land as elder kin) rests.
figures.provenance: Malo; Kamakau; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. on Wākea and Papa; the Hāloa genealogy as recorded in 19th-c. Hawaiian-language newspaper accounts.

figures.gloss: The stillborn first child of Wākea and Hoʻohōkūkalani, buried at the house corner, from whose body the first taro plant (Hāloa-naka) springs; his living younger brother, named Hāloa in his memory, is the first man and ancestor of the Hawaiian people — humankind as the taro's younger sibling, owing it care in return for food.
figures.facet: The charter of an entire land ethic told as a kinship diagram rather than a commandment: because kalo is literally the elder brother, tending it is filial duty, not stewardship imposed from outside — mālama ʻāina follows automatically from the genealogy, not from a separate law.
figures.provenance: Kamakau, 'Ka Poʻe Kahiko'; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology'; discussed at length in modern Hawaiian cultural-education literature (e.g. Kanahele, Kameʻeleihiwa) as the foundational aloha-ʻāina narrative.

figures.gloss: Deified family ancestors who take on animal or elemental bodies — shark, owl, moʻo, stone, or cloud — and continue to watch over their living descendants: the shark that turns a swimmer from danger, the owl that warns of an ambush. Fed, honored, and never harmed by the families that claim them; neglect can turn an ʻaumakua's favor to abandonment.
figures.facet: Guardianship here is ongoing and transactional rather than a one-time origin: the ʻaumākua are the everyday, working layer of Hawaiian religion — not distant creators but managing kin who require continued feeding and correct behavior to keep acting on the family's behalf.
figures.provenance: E. S. Craighill Handy & Mary Kawena Pukui, 'The Polynesian Family System in Kaʻu, Hawaiʻi' (1958); Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. on aumakua worship; Pukui, Haertig & Lee, 'Nānā i ke Kumu' (1972).

figures.gloss: Ruler of Lua-o-Milu, the subterranean realm of the dead, to which souls (ʻuhane) depart by leaping from designated cliffs and precipices (na leina a ka ʻuhane) at Kaʻena Point, Waipiʻo, and elsewhere. In some tellings a chief of Waipiʻo whose own misdeeds and defiance of the healing gods led to his banishment below, where the dead pursue shadow versions of the same games and pleasures as the living.
figures.facet: The underworld is a mirror world rather than a punishment: Milu's dead surf, gamble, and feast much as they did in life, so Hawaiian afterlife belief locates continuity of ordinary pleasure below ground rather than reward or torment.
figures.provenance: Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. 'The Soul After Death'; Fornander Collection texts on Milu and the leina a ka ʻuhane; the Lua-o-Milu place-tradition recorded across Oʻahu and Hawaiʻi Island sources.

figures.gloss: Goddess of the hula and of the forest plants — maile, ʻieʻie, palapalai, lehua — gathered to dress the kuahu (altar) maintained in every hālau hula; invoked by chant before instruction and performance begin, her presence is what makes the training hall sacred ground rather than a mere rehearsal space.
figures.facet: An entire performing art is organized around a single household altar: Laka's cult shows how Hawaiian religion could concentrate around a specific craft-guild practice (the hālau) rather than only around temple or family worship.
figures.provenance: Nathaniel Emerson, 'Unwritten Literature of Hawaii: The Sacred Songs of the Hula' (1909); Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. on Laka; modern hālau practice as documented in Amy Kaʻōhelo Stillman's scholarship on hula.

figures.gloss: The snow-mantled goddess who dwells on the summit of Mauna Kea, attended by sister goddesses of cold and mist; her contests with Pele over territory and, in some tellings, over a suitor, are told as the reason the highest peak stays crowned in snow above the fire-pits of Kīlauea.
figures.facet: Fire and ice are given equal standing as goddesses rather than fire alone being personified and cold left as mere absence: Poliahu makes the volcanic archipelago's coldest point as actively willed as its hottest.
figures.provenance: Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. on Poliahu and the snow goddesses; the Poliahu-Pele contest narratives recorded in 19th-c. Hawaiian-language newspaper moʻolelo.

figures.gloss: Giant black lizard-spirits, sometimes many yards long, inhabiting fishponds, streams, and cliff pools as guardians or devourers; several are Hiʻiaka's chief adversaries on her journey to Kauaʻi, while others — such as the moʻo-queen traditions associated with royal fishponds — are honored as ʻaumākua and protectors by particular families.
figures.facet: The same monster class is cast as both threat and protector depending on relationship: an unrelated moʻo devours travelers, but a family's own moʻo ʻaumakua guards its fishpond — Hawaiian monster-lore runs on kinship, not a fixed good/evil register.
figures.provenance: Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. on moʻo; the Pele-and-Hiʻiaka epic's recurring moʻo antagonists (Emerson 1915; Hoʻoulumāhiehie); Valerio Valeri and later ethnohistorians on moʻo-guarded royal fishponds.

figures.gloss: A legendary race of small, skilled laborers said to complete great works of stonework — fishponds, heiau walls, irrigation ditches — in a single night, passing stone hand to hand in a human chain and vanishing before daybreak if the work is not finished by dawn; credited in local tradition with structures such as the Alekoko (Menehune) Fishpond on Kauaʻi and the Menehune Ditch at Waimea.
figures.facet: Real, dateable stonework is explained by attaching it to a vanished, magically efficient labor force: the Menehune legend lets attested engineering stand as evidence of a lost people, folk archaeology working in the same register as myth.
figures.provenance: Thrum, 'Hawaiian Folk Tales', the Menehune chapter; Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology'; Katharine Luomala, 'Menehune of Polynesia and Other Mythical Little People of Oceania' (1951), the standard scholarly treatment.

figures.gloss: Ghostly processions of deified aliʻi and warrior spirits who march with torchlight and drum on sacred nights along old trails and battle routes; the living are warned to flee, hide, or lie face-down and silent, since to be seen looking upon the march risks death unless an ancestor among the marchers intervenes on the viewer's behalf.
figures.facet: Protection here depends entirely on genealogical luck rather than ritual technique: survival of an encounter is said to hinge on whether one happens to have a marching ancestor willing to plead for them, making the belief a live test of one's own lineage rather than a defense anyone can perform.
figures.provenance: Recorded continuously from 19th-c. Hawaiian-language newspaper accounts through 20th-c. folklore collections (e.g. Pukui & Green, 'Folktales of Hawaii'); a widely and currently held living belief across the islands.

figures.gloss: Goddess of fertility and safe childbirth, credited with repeatedly renewing her youth and bearing an extraordinary number of children and gods — among them, in the Pele-cycle genealogies, Pele, Hiʻiaka, Kamapuaʻa, and Kapo; in some genealogical chants she is identified outright with Papa, in others kept as a distinct, more purely generative figure.
figures.facet: Fertility is decoupled from aging: Haumea's signature miracle is not fecundity alone but the capacity to shed old age and bear again, making her the tradition's clearest statement that the generative principle need not itself decline.
figures.provenance: Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. on Haumea; the Haumea genealogical chant recorded by Kamakau and in Fornander Collection texts; her identification with Papa is discussed as a genealogical variant rather than a settled equivalence.

figures.gloss: Goddess of sorcery, fertility ritual, and dark powers, sister of Pele, Hiʻiaka, and Kamapuaʻa in the Haumea genealogy; famed for possessing a detachable, self-directing vagina (ka wahine lele) which she flings ahead as a decoy to lure the pursuing Kamapuaʻa away and save Pele — the crater Koko Head (Kohelepelepe) is named for its landing place.
figures.facet: A rescue accomplished by body-as-weapon rather than combat: Kapo's decoy resolves the Pele-Kamapuaʻa crisis through sorcerous misdirection, giving the cycle its one clean instance of guile outmaneuvering a physically superior antagonist.
figures.provenance: Beckwith, 'Hawaiian Mythology', ch. on Kapo; the Kohelepelepe (Koko Head) place-legend recorded in Sterling & Summers, 'Sites of Oʻahu'; the Pele-Kamapuaʻa cycle (Thrum 1907).

edges.note: The chant IS the unfolding of Pō: the Kumulipo's first seven wā are the generations born within the Night before the dawn of Ao — the genealogy and the dark source it climbs out of are one cosmogonic act.

Named in one breath and travelling the islands together opening freshwater springs — Kāne the sweet water and light, Kanaloa the salt deep and dark; the bound creative dyad at the heart of Hawaiian theology.

The calendar's two halves embodied: Kū's season of war and human sacrifice against Lono's Makahiki of peace, sport, and plenty — the same forceful and fruitful world alternating under two opposed gods.
Fire-sister and sea-sister locked in the founding quarrel: Nāmakaokahaʻi's salt water drives Pele from Kahiki and forever chases her lava — the volcano and the ocean staged as a sibling feud.
In many Hawaiian tellings Hina is the mother (or grandmother) of Māui; the moon-woman bears the island-fishing trickster — the great feminine and the small demigod bound in one genealogy.
A chief's mana is his measured place in the Kumulipo — nearness to Pō and the gods read straight off the genealogy; the sacred-power system and the cosmic chant are the same ranking of descent.
Pele's own family is set in genealogical order like everything else — her line, her sisters and the youngest Hiʻiaka are a whakapapa within the larger one; even the fire-goddess is placed by descent.

Kāne IS Tāne by regular sound-law: Proto-Polynesian *Taane > Hawaiian Kāne under the systematic shift *t > Hawaiian k. The same inherited creator-and-light god, name and role retained, split across the Polynesian languages.

Kanaloa < Proto-Polynesian *Taŋaroa by exact reflex — *t > k, *ŋ > n, *r > l — the same ocean-god as Māori Tangaroa / Samoan Tagaloa; cognate of the deep, with the Hawaiian peculiarity that he is Kāne's companion rather than a paramount sky-source.

Kū continues Proto-Polynesian *Tuu (*t > Hawaiian k); Māori Tū(-matauenga) is the same inherited god of war and the upright — the Hawaiian war-god and the Māori war-god are reflexes of one Polynesian etymon.

Lono < Proto-Polynesian *Rongo by regular reflex — *r > l, *ŋ > n — the cognate of Māori Rongo, god of cultivated food and peace; the same agriculture-and-peace deity descended in two daughter languages.

One and the same hero by descent: Proto-Polynesian *Maaui, the demigod who fishes up islands, snares the sun, and steals fire, shared across Polynesia with extraordinary fidelity — the Hawaiian and the wider-Polynesian Māui are the identical figure.

The cosmos issuing whole from a single dark generative source — Pō the teeming primordial Night against the world-egg of Orphic, Finnish, and Dogon thought; both make the origin a fertile enclosure that gives birth from within rather than a chaos subdued.

Both the light, sky-associated, life-giving pole of the Polynesian creative pair — Kāne of the day and fresh water beside the Māori Sky-Father Ranginui; near-kin theologies of the bright above, though Hawaiian thought makes Kāne a maker more than a parted world-parent.

A faint structural rhyme only: both are the dark, lower, receiving pole of a Polynesian dyad — Kanaloa's deep sea against Papatūānuku the Earth-Mother below — but Kanaloa is sea not earth and male not mother; the depth-and-darkness pairing shared, the figures otherwise unalike, flagged.

The fire-bringer to humankind reached in two unconnected worlds — Māui snatching fire from the mud-hens or Mahuika for people, Prometheus stealing it from the gods; the trickster culture-hero who steals the means of cooking and is punished for it.

Both fish a wondrous thing up out of the primal sea with hook and line — Māui hauling the islands up, Väinämöinen drawing up the great pike (and the egg-world made on the water) — a loose angling-cosmogony echo, flagged.

The great-mother who both brings forth and devours — Pele who builds the land in the same eruption that buries it, Coatlicue the earth-mother of birth and the maw; the creative-destructive feminine ground reached on either side of the Pacific.

Both ground a living, volcanically active homeland in a goddess of the earth itself — Pele in Kīlauea's pit, Pachamama in the Andean ground that shakes — but Pachamama is the nurturing tilled earth where Pele is fire and fury; a loose earth-goddess rhyme, flagged.

The feminine seated in the moon — Hina who climbed the moonbeam to beat her kapa there, Mama Killa the Inca silver Moon-mother; the lunar goddess as the bright female counter to the sun, imagined in unrelated worlds.

Same celestial body, opposite gender: Hina is the WOMAN in the moon where Baltic Mėnulis is the Moon-husband to Saulė the Sun-wife — the moon sexed female in Polynesia, male in the Baltic; structure shared, valence flipped, flagged.

The god of growth who departs and is awaited — Lono who goes over the sea and returns with the winter rains of the Makahiki, Dumuzi the shepherd who dies into the underworld and rises with the season; the vegetation-deity's annual leaving and return.

Two names for a sacred power diffused in differing measure through persons, places, and things — Hawaiian mana and Iroquois orenda; the holy as an efficacious potency rather than a personal god, intuited independently across the Pacific and North America.
Mana beside Turkic-Mongol kut — both a transmissible sacred fortune/power that gods bestow and that legitimates the ruler; the holy charge of chiefship and luck, reached in two unconnected worlds.

Mana and Nahua teōtl both name a pervasive sacred energy filling the cosmos rather than a single high god — the impersonal holy power-stuff that manifests in differing concentrations; the same sacred-power intuition on opposite shores of the Pacific.

The kapu as a Hawaiian face of impersonal cosmic law — the system of sacred prohibition that keeps mana and the world in right separation, kin in function to Ṛta, Maʿat, and hózhó, holiness made into binding order.

GOLD SEAM: the genealogical cosmogony as a shared East Polynesian inherited FORM — the Kumulipo is literally a whakapapa of the cosmos; the chants are independent compositions, but the genre of world-as-recited-descent is common ancestry.

Proven descent: Māori tapu and Hawaiian kapu are regular reflexes of Proto-Polynesian *tapu (*t > Hawaiian k), and mana is the same inherited word in both — one sacred-power-and-prohibition system split across the daughter languages.
Two namings of where the dead return: the Hawaiian dead go back into Pō, the generative Night, as the Māori dead voyage back to Hawaiki — the source-realm doubling as destination in both theologies.

The devoted youngest sister carried to Hawaiʻi as an egg in Pele's own breast; the entire Pele-and-Hiʻiaka epic is staged as a debt of love and jealousy between the two, the tradition's central sibling bond.

The moʻo are Hiʻiaka's recurring adversaries on the road to Kauaʻi — her chants and fire subdue or slay them at nearly every stage of the journey, the epic's default monster-and-heroine confrontation.
Rain-lush hog-god against fire-goddess: their storied war and courtship across the islands stages the wet windward/dry leeward division of Hawaiʻi as an elemental romance-and-battle cycle.
Kapo's flying decoy vagina lures Kamapuaʻa away from Pele at Koko Head — sorcerous misdirection defeating the pursuing hog-god where force could not.
Sisters in the Haumea genealogy; Kapo's decoy trick is performed specifically to save Pele from Kamapuaʻa, the clearest act of sibling protection in the cycle after Hiʻiaka's own.
Haumea is named as mother or ancestress of the Pele-cycle siblings (Pele, Hiʻiaka, Kamapuaʻa, Kapo) in the genealogical chants that frame the family's origin.

Some genealogical chants identify Haumea directly with Papa the earth-mother, while others keep them distinct generative figures; the overlap itself is attested in the sources, not merely a modern conflation.

The world-parent couple of the second great cosmogonic strand: Papa the earth and Wākea the sky, whose union begins the chiefly genealogies and whose separation opens the space the myths otherwise leave unstated.

Wākea fathers the stillborn taro-child and the living Hāloa on Hoʻohōkūkalani; the sky-father's affair, not his marriage, is the myth's load-bearing act, founding both the kalo and the human chiefly line.

Milu's realm, Lua-o-Milu, is a personal, ruled underworld reached by leaping from named cliffs — a bounded destination within, or beside, the vast generative Pō rather than identical to it; sources vary on whether the two are the same darkness.

Ancestral guardians operate within the same mana economy as chiefly rank: an ʻaumakua's protective power is itself a form of mana that must be maintained through correct feeding and behavior, on the same logic that governs kapu.

Snow-goddess of the summit against fire-goddess of the pit: their territorial and romantic rivalry is the tradition's second great elemental antagonism, distinct from the Pele/Nāmaka sea-and-fire feud.

Both are patronesses of the hula and its plant-dressed altar in different strands of practice — classically distinct goddesses, but invoked together or interchangeably in a number of modern hālau traditions.

The sky-father half of the same East Polynesian World-Parents complex — Wākea paired with Papa as Ranginui is paired with Papatūānuku — the identical mythic structure under a different inherited term for 'sky/space' in each daughter culture.

Papa continues Proto-Polynesian *papa ('flat rock, foundation, earth') without sound change in either daughter language: the same inherited word and the same earth-mother role, split across Hawaiian and Māori.

Both a personal, named ruler of a below-ground land of the dead reached by a fixed threshold — Milu's Lua-o-Milu via the leina a ka ʻuhane, Erlen Khan's underworld via its own passage — a loose structural rhyme between unrelated traditions, flagged.

Both cast the honored dead as ongoing active guardians embodied in animal or elemental form, fed and consulted by descendants rather than left to a single origin-point veneration — the active-intercessor pattern reached independently in Polynesia and southern Africa.
Two Polynesian earth-violence deities — Rūaumoko's earthquakes as Papatūānuku's unweaned child turning in her womb, Pele's eruptions as her own volcanic body — kin in theme, not in descent.

The same Oceanic term and force, differently institutionalized: Codrington's Melanesian mana is impersonal efficacy residing in things and acts, while Hawaiian mana is rank-bound potency inherited in chiefly blood and hedged by the kapu system's prohibitions.

Same Proto-Oceanic root *tapu by regular sound-law descent (Hawaiian kapu, Banks Islands tabu, source of English 'taboo'), but sharply different constitutional weight: a flexible marker any chief or mana-holder can lay in Melanesia versus Hawaii's state-enforced ritual law whose breach was capital.

dossier.essence: The Polynesian religion of the Hawaiian Islands, carried by voyagers who settled the archipelago roughly 1000–1200 CE and elaborated in isolation into the classic system that the deliberate breaking of the eating-tabu in 1819 ended as a state cult — now the ground of a living cultural and religious revival. It worships a great company of gods led by four — Kāne, Kanaloa, Kū, and Lono — reveres the volcano-goddess Pele, and orders all of life by mana (sacred power) and kapu (sacred prohibition).
dossier.cosmos: The world is not made but begotten: the Kumulipo, a chant of some 2,000 lines, unfolds the cosmos out of Pō, the deep primordial Night, beginning with the coral polyp and climbing through paired sea- and land-life to the gods and finally the chiefs — so coral, pig, commoner, and king are ranked kin in one descent. Kāne (light, fresh water, the living) and Kanaloa (the ocean deep) form a bound creative pair; Kū (war and the upright) and Lono (rain, agriculture, peace) split the year between them. The dead return into Pō and to the ancestral guardians ('aumākua).
dossier.practice: A ranked society of chiefs (aliʻi), commoners (makaʻāinana), and priests (kahuna) maintained the gods at temples (heiau) — the luakini war-temples of Kū requiring human sacrifice, the firstfruits and awa-offerings of Kāne and Lono asking none. The kapu system governed daily life by sacred separation (men's and women's eating, the seasons, the chief's very shadow), and the four-month Makahiki festival of Lono suspended war for tribute, sport, and renewal; family religion honored the 'aumākua through chant, healing, and hula.
dossier.canon: Oral and chanted: the Kumulipo (set down and translated by Queen Liliʻuokalani in 1897 and annotated by Martha Beckwith in 1951); the Pele-and-Hiʻiaka epic; and the 19th-century Hawaiian-language works of native scholars David Malo ('Hawaiian Antiquities'), Samuel Kamakau, and Kepelino, who preserved the old religion after its official fall.
dossier.reflects: Rank as a sacred physics — a cosmos where genealogy is destiny, holiness (mana) is a real and graded power proportional to one's nearness in descent to the gods, and taboo (kapu) is the engineering that keeps that power safe.
sources: Valerio Valeri, 'Kingship and Sacrifice: Ritual and Society in Ancient Hawaii' (1985)
